attributes.stem:
A 75-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic for review of his chronic lower back pain, which he has experienced for eight years. The pain is constant, worse with prolonged sitting, and improved with gentle movement. Previous magnetic resonance imaging of his spine showed only age-appropriate degenerative changes without nerve root compression. He has a medical history of hypertension, osteoarthritis of both knees, and depression. His medications include perindopril, paracetamol, and sertraline. He lives alone, is independent with activities of daily living, but has reduced his walking due to fear of worsening pain.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. He has a slow, antalgic gait and appears anxious about movement. There is generalised tenderness over the lumbar paraspinal muscles without focal neurological deficits. Straight leg raise is negative bilaterally. Hip and knee range of motion is preserved.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate counselling approach?
options: Pursue further imaging to identify source of pain
Steroid injection to most affected area
Continue current medications and accept declining function
Focus on gradually increasing activity despite pain
Avoid all physical activity until the pain resolves
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with chronic lower back pain without concerning features, demonstrating features of pain-related fear and deconditioning. The management approach should focus on addressing these barriers to recovery and improving function.

Focus on gradually increasing activity despite pain is the most appropriate approach. Exercise therapy and graduated activity are the cornerstones of chronic back pain management. Patient education should emphasise that movement is safe and beneficial, even if it temporarily increases pain. This approach helps break the cycle of fear-avoidance and deconditioning.

Avoid all physical activity until the pain resolves is inappropriate as it reinforces fear-avoidance behaviour and can lead to further deconditioning. Complete rest can worsen chronic pain by promoting muscle weakness and joint stiffness.

Continue current medications and accept declining function represents an overly passive approach. While medications may help manage symptoms, accepting functional decline contradicts the goal of maintaining independence and quality of life. Active management strategies are essential for preventing further deterioration.

Pursue further imaging is not indicated as previous imaging has already shown only age-appropriate changes. Additional imaging in chronic pain without new symptoms or red flags does not improve outcomes and may increase anxiety about structural changes that are normal for age.

Steroid injection to the most affected area is not appropriate as first-line therapy for non-specific chronic back pain. Without specific pathology or radicular symptoms, injections are unlikely to provide long-term benefit and carry risks, particularly in elderly patients.

The management of chronic back pain should focus on a biopsychosocial approach. This includes addressing fear-avoidance behaviours, gradually increasing activity levels, and setting realistic goals for functional improvement. Pain neuroscience education helps patients understand that pain does not always indicate tissue damage and that movement is generally safe and beneficial.
learningPoints: Exercise and physical activity remain the cornerstone of chronic lower back pain management, even in elderly patients.
Patient education about pain neuroscience and the biopsychosocial model improves outcomes in chronic back pain.
Chronic back pain management should focus on function and quality of life rather than complete pain elimination.
references: Buchbinder, Rachellea,b,*; Underwood, Martinc,d; Hartvigsen, Jane,f; Maher, Chris G.g,h. The Lancet Series call to action to reduce low value care for low back pain: an update. PAIN 161():p S57-S64, September 2020. | DOI: 10.1097/j.pain.0000000000001869. Available online at ; https://journals.lww.com/pain/fulltext/2020/09001/The_Lancet_Series_call_to_action_to_reduce_low.7.aspx?fbclid=IwAR1wPrLABWzKjZmHaV8XOfrKYW65RQ33r2UjvRXdJ0TDnZ0E1owJc-zHr_U
